annotations.text: HIV infection was first diagnosed when this child was admitted to the hospital with pneumonia at 5 years 10 months of age. Although antiretroviral therapy was initiated, there are several possible reasons why it was only partially effective.

Lack of adherence to the therapeutic regimen, the specific choice and dosing of antiretroviral drugs, and possible interactions with the antimycobacterial agents may all have contributed to the lack of therapeutic response in this patient.
Poor adherence to antiretroviral therapy is the most common reason for virologic failure.

A combination of three drugs from these classes, usually consisting of two NRTIs combined with either a protease inhibitor or an NNRTI, provides optimal suppression of viral replication and lowers the risk of the acquisition of drug resistance.
The risk of mutations depends on both the duration of exposure to the drug and the duration of treatment failure.

The area under the curve (AUC) for the response to ritonavir is reduced by up to 35% with the addition of rifampicin, so ritonavir is generally not recommended for use concomitantly with tuberculosis treatment.

This event may have been due to the immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS), which is associated with tuberculosis, since the abscess occurred shortly after antiretroviral therapy was started and responded to continued tuberculosis treatment.
The patient had HIV subtype C; the viral sequences showed mutations associated with multidrug resistance.

The mutations associated with high-level resistance to protease inhibitors were most likely a result of ineffective treatment with ritonavir, and the many mutations associated with thymidine analogues suggested prolonged use of a partially effective NRTI regime.
In patients with multiple mutations associated with resistance to older protease-inhibitors, ritonavir-boosted darunavir or tipranavir may still be useful.
Since there are no NNRTI mutations, efavirenz is still an active agent and is readily available through the antiretroviral treatment program of the South African government.

Families may initially be resistant to telling the child. They may feel guilty about having transmitted the infection to the child, and they are often worried that the child might disclose the diagnosis to others.

Especially with young children, one may not need to use the term HIV but could instead tell them that they have an infection in their blood for which they need to take medication, and that if they get a cut, they need to clean up the blood themselves.
